items.correct: Their account is undermined within the text by ignorance, bias, self-deception or deceit, so the reader must read against what they say.
items.misconception: An unreliable narrator is one who tells lies in the story.
items.random_wrong: An unreliable narrator is when the author makes mistakes.
items.why: Unreliability is structural — the text gives signals the narrator is wrong (e.g. internal contradictions). Lying intentionally is one kind, but blind spots and self-deception count too.

items.q: Why must students distinguish the narrator from the author?
items.correct: The narrator is a constructed voice within the text; the author is the real person who created it. Conflating them misreads irony, unreliable narration and persona.
items.misconception: The narrator's views are always the author's views.
items.random_wrong: The narrator and author are different words for the same thing.
items.why: Students attribute everything a first-person narrator says to the author. This collapses irony — a novel like 'Lolita' cannot work if you take Humbert as Nabokov.

items.q: What is the difference between a theme and a symbol?
items.correct: A theme is an abstract idea or concern the text explores (e.g. mortality); a symbol is a concrete image that stands for something else (e.g. a skull).
items.misconception: Themes are just symbols repeated through the book.
items.random_wrong: A theme is the title of the book.
items.why: Students treat theme and symbol as the same thing. Themes are ideas, symbols are images. Skulls (symbol) can develop the theme (mortality), but they are not the same level of abstraction.

items.q: In which year was Ian McEwan's Atonement first published?
items.why: It's 2001. “1998” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In the 1999 coda, what does Briony reveal about Robbie and Cecilia's reunion?
items.correct: She invented it; they both died before meeting again
items.random_wrong: It happened but they later separated
items.why: The answer examiners want is: She invented it; they both died before meeting again. “It happened exactly as written” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In which year is Part One of Atonement set?
items.why: The real figure is 1935. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
